text: The surgeon marks the visceral peritoneum from the ileum to the mesenteric region, using the superior mesenteric vein as a reference. Dissection proceeds below the ileocolic vessels, focusing on the superior mesenteric vein.

The surgeon performs a D-free lymphadenectomy, removing lymphoadipose tissue from the anterior surface of the superior mesenteric vein, including the surgical trunk of Guillot. With the assistant maintaining traction on the ileocolic pedicle, the surgeon enters the embryological plane to separate the mesocolon from retroperitoneal structures, progressing the dissection laterally and cranially. The right mesocolon is fully liberated from the retroperitoneum, followed by dissection of the ileocolic pedicle.

The surgeon encounters the ileocolic vein and artery, performing central ligation of these vessels. The dissection continues along the surgical trunk of Guillot on the anterior surface of the superior mesenteric vein. The section of the ileocolic vessels facilitates the separation of the duodenum and pancreas from the mesocolon, following the embryological plane of Fredet.

A gauze is placed to mark the reference point for the cranial approach to the middle colic vessels. The surgeon sections the gastrocolic ligament, entering the epiploic transcavity, with the right gastroepiploic vessels serving as a visual reference. Part of the gastrocolic ligament is included in the surgical specimen.

The surgeon performs lateral dissection, sectioning the collohepatic and right colophrenic ligaments to release the hepatic flexure. The cranial approach of the middle colic vessels is followed, separating the right gastroepiploic vessels from the transverse mesocolon until they enter the gastrocolic trunk of Henle. A gauze is placed to separate the dissection areas, serving as a reference for completing the deep-free lymphadenectomy.

The surgeon creates a window at the inferior pancreatic border to separate the transverse mesocolon from its posterior attachments, facilitating vascular dissection. The D-free lymphadenectomy is performed along the anterior surface of the superior mesenteric vein. The gastrocolic trunk of Henle is dissected, with identification and sectioning of its branches, including the right superior colic vein and middle colic vein, which drain into the trunk.

The surgeon performs the dissection and central ligation of the middle colic artery, followed by the central ligation of the middle colic vein. The transverse mesocolon is immobilized by sectioning its pancreatic attachments.

The surgical area is shown following a D-free lymphadenectomy during an extended right hemicolectomy. The superior mesenteric vein is fully exposed as the lymphoadipose tissue surrounding it is included in the surgical specimen. The transverse colon is sectioned after confirming vascularization with indocyanine green. The procedure concludes with an intracorporeal isoperistaltic lateral-lateral mechanical ileocolic anastomosis, and the enterotomy is closed using a barbed suture.

The histopathology report confirms the quality of the surgical specimen, based on the recently introduced criteria for right hemicolectomy. The D-free area lymph nodes are analyzed separately, in this case none of them was infiltrated by the tumor.
